events.text: Patient is a 77-year-old White male, height 5'8", weight 172 lbs, BMI 26.2, former smoker with 30 pack-year history, history of hypertension and atrial fibrillation on apixaban, no family history of prostate cancer.
At age 70, patient presented with severe low back pain and was found to have a PSA of 84 ng/mL; prostate biopsy confirmed adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3).

At age 70, transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy showed adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 5+5=10 (Grade Group 5), involving all 12 cores with extensive perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion, and areas of comedonecrosis.

At age 70, bone scan revealed diffuse skeletal metastases involving the axial and appendicular skeleton including calvarium, multiple vertebrae, bilateral ribs, pelvis, and bilateral proximal femora; described as a superscan pattern.

At age 70, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed extensive sclerotic bone metastases, bilateral retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (largest 3.2 cm), and two subcentimeter pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe indeterminate for metastases.

At age 70, patient was staged as cT4N1M1b-c adenocarcinoma of the prostate with de novo metastatic disease involving extensive bone metastases, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and possible pulmonary metastases.
At age 70, goserelin 10.8 mg subcutaneous every 3 months was initiated along with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg daily and prednisone 5 mg daily for de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer.
At age 70, patient developed grade 2 hypertension (BP 168/98 mmHg) and grade 2 peripheral edema on abiraterone/prednisone; diuretic and antihypertensive regimen was adjusted.
At age 70, after 3 months of combined therapy, PSA declined from 84 to 12.4 ng/mL; back pain improved significantly with ADT and radiation.
At age 70, palliative radiation therapy 30 Gy in 10 fractions was delivered to the lumbar spine (L2-L5) for severe pain and concern for cord compression.
At age 71, PSA nadir was 4.2 ng/mL on goserelin plus abiraterone; testosterone was 11 ng/dL.
At age 72, restaging bone scan and CT showed stable diffuse bone disease and stable pulmonary nodules; retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy decreased to 1.8 cm.
At age 73, PSA rose to 18.6 ng/mL on abiraterone with testosterone of 16 ng/dL; castration-resistant progression confirmed.
At age 73, disease reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with progressive disease on abiraterone plus ADT.
At age 73, abiraterone was discontinued and docetaxel 75 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was initiated.

At age 73, next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT) on fresh biopsy of right iliac bone metastasis revealed TP53 R175H and R248W biallelic mutations, RB1 loss, PTEN homozygous deletion, AR amplification and AR-V7 splice variant detected; no BRCA1/2 or HRD mutations; tumor mutational burden 8 mutations/Mb; microsatellite stable.

At age 73, CT-guided biopsy of right iliac bone metastasis showed high-grade adenocarcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation; positive for PSA, PSAP, and focal synaptophysin staining by immunohistochemistry; Ki-67 proliferation index was 45%.
At age 73, after cycle 2 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 500/uL), grade 2 fatigue, and grade 2 peripheral neuropathy; dose reduced to 60 mg/m2 with pegfilgrastim support.
At age 74, CT showed enlargement of pulmonary nodules to 1.8 cm and 1.2 cm confirmed as metastatic disease, progressive bone metastases, and new 2.1 cm hepatic lesion; PSA was 42 ng/mL.
At age 74, patient reported grade 3 bone pain requiring hospitalization and initiation of patient-controlled analgesia with hydromorphone; palliative care was consulted.
At age 74, cabazitaxel 20 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks was initiated after disease progression on docetaxel.
At age 75, patient developed grade 3 diarrhea after cycle 1 of cabazitaxel requiring IV fluids and dose reduction to 15 mg/m2.
At age 77, PSA is 88 ng/mL on cabazitaxel with diffuse progressive disease; best supportive care and hospice referral were discussed given declining performance status (ECOG 3).

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
